description: A common viral infection of the upper respiratory tract caused mainly by rhinoviruses. Usually mild and resolves within 7-10 days.
precautions: Wash hands frequently to prevent spreading
whenToSeeDoctor: If fever exceeds 39°C (102°F), symptoms last more than 10 days, difficulty breathing, severe headache or sinus pain.
dietAdvice: Warm fluids, chicken soup, honey with warm water, vitamin C rich foods

description: A contagious respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses. More severe than the common cold with sudden onset of symptoms.
precautions: Stay home and rest for at least 24 hours after fever breaks
Cover mouth and nose when coughing or sneezing
whenToSeeDoctor: Difficulty breathing, chest pain, persistent vomiting, confusion, severe or persistent vomiting, symptoms improve then return with fever.
dietAdvice: Clear broths, electrolyte drinks, soft easily digestible foods

description: A chronic condition where blood pressure in the arteries is persistently elevated. Often called the 'silent killer' as it may have no symptoms.
precautions: Reduce salt intake to less than 5g per day
Manage stress through meditation or yoga
whenToSeeDoctor: BP consistently above 140/90 mmHg, severe headache, vision problems, chest pain, difficulty breathing, or irregular heartbeat.
dietAdvice: DASH diet: fruits, vegetables, low-fat dairy, whole grains, lean protein. Avoid high sodium processed foods.

description: A metabolic disorder characterized by high blood glucose levels due to insulin resistance or insufficient insulin production.
precautions: Follow a low glycemic index diet
Exercise for at least 30 minutes daily
Avoid sugary drinks and processed foods
whenToSeeDoctor: Blood sugar consistently above 250 mg/dL, symptoms of hypoglycemia (shakiness, confusion), foot ulcers, vision changes.
dietAdvice: Low GI foods, whole grains, leafy vegetables, lean proteins. Avoid refined sugars, white bread, sugary beverages.

description: A mosquito-borne viral infection causing severe flu-like illness. Can develop into severe dengue (hemorrhagic fever) which can be life-threatening.

medicines.name: Paracetamol
medicines.warning: DO NOT use Aspirin or Ibuprofen — they increase bleeding risk

whenToSeeDoctor: IMMEDIATELY if platelet count falls below 100,000, bleeding from gums or nose, blood in urine/stool, severe abdominal pain, rapid breathing.
dietAdvice: Papaya leaf juice (may help platelets), coconut water, fresh fruits, light digestible foods. Stay well hydrated.

description: A life-threatening disease caused by Plasmodium parasites transmitted through infected mosquito bites. Requires prompt treatment.
precautions: Seek immediate medical care if symptoms appear
whenToSeeDoctor: ALWAYS see a doctor immediately for suspected malaria. Seek emergency care for confusion, seizures, difficulty breathing, or jaundice.
dietAdvice: Light easily digestible foods, plenty of fluids, avoid fatty foods during fever phases.

description: A bacterial infection caused by Salmonella typhi, spread through contaminated food and water. Can be severe without treatment.
precautions: Drink only boiled or bottled water
Wash hands before eating and after toilet
Avoid raw vegetables and street food
whenToSeeDoctor: All suspected typhoid cases need medical attention. Emergency: intestinal perforation symptoms (severe abdominal pain, rigid abdomen), severe bleeding.
dietAdvice: Soft, easily digestible foods: khichdi, curd rice, banana, boiled vegetables. Avoid spicy, oily, high-fiber foods.

description: A chronic inflammatory disease of the airways causing recurrent episodes of wheezing, breathlessness, and coughing, often triggered by allergens or exercise.
precautions: Identify and avoid triggers (dust, pollen, smoke)

medicines.name: Salbutamol (Albuterol) Inhaler
medicines.duration: As needed (not more than 4 times daily)

whenToSeeDoctor: Inhaler not helping within minutes, lips turning blue, difficulty speaking, severe breathlessness at rest.
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory diet: fruits, vegetables, nuts. Avoid sulfites (in wine, dried fruits), aspirin if sensitive.

description: A neurological condition characterized by intense, debilitating headaches often accompanied by sensory disturbances. Can last 4-72 hours.
precautions: Identify and avoid personal triggers (stress, certain foods, bright lights)
Rest in dark, quiet room during attack

medicines.name: Sumatriptan (Imigran)
medicines.frequency: At onset, may repeat after 2 hours

whenToSeeDoctor: Sudden worst headache of life (thunderclap), headache with fever/stiff neck, headache after head injury, progressive worsening over days.
dietAdvice: Avoid triggers: aged cheese, red wine, chocolate, caffeine (or ensure consistent intake). Regular meal times important.

description: Inflammation of the stomach and intestines caused by viral, bacterial, or parasitic infections, often from contaminated food or water.
precautions: Rest the gut with light foods
Avoid dairy, fatty, spicy foods temporarily

medicines.name: Loperamide (Imodium)
medicines.adultDose: 4mg initially then 2mg after each loose stool

whenToSeeDoctor: Blood in stool or vomit, severe dehydration (no urination for 8 hours), fever above 39°C, symptoms lasting more than 3 days, diarrhea in infants.
dietAdvice: BRAT diet: Bananas, Rice, Applesauce, Toast. Gradually return to normal diet. Avoid dairy, caffeine, alcohol.

description: An infectious disease caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus. Most people experience mild to moderate illness; severe disease occurs in older adults and those with underlying health conditions.
precautions: Wear N95 mask if around others
Monitor oxygen levels with pulse oximeter
Ventilate rooms and maintain fresh air
whenToSeeDoctor: Oxygen saturation below 94%, difficulty breathing, persistent chest pain, confusion, bluish lips or face, high fever not responding to medication.
dietAdvice: High protein foods to aid recovery, vitamin C and zinc rich foods, warm fluids, avoid processed foods. Turmeric milk is beneficial.

description: A highly contagious viral infection caused by varicella-zoster virus, characterized by itchy blister rash. Common in children but can be severe in adults.
precautions: Stay home until all blisters have crusted over
Avoid scratching to prevent scarring and infection
Avoid contact with pregnant women, newborns, and immunocompromised individuals

medicines.name: Calamine Lotion
medicines.adultDose: Apply to affected areas 3-4 times daily

whenToSeeDoctor: High fever over 39°C, rash spreading to eyes, severe skin infection from scratching, difficulty breathing, confusion, or stiff neck.
dietAdvice: Soft, non-spicy foods if mouth sores develop. Cold yogurt, ice cream help soothe mouth lesions. Stay well hydrated.

description: A bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, primarily affecting the lungs. A leading infectious disease killer worldwide but treatable with a full course of antibiotics.
precautions: Complete the full 6-month antibiotic course without skipping doses
All close contacts should be tested
Avoid public places during infectious period
whenToSeeDoctor: Any suspicion of TB requires immediate doctor consultation. Do not self-medicate. TB is a notifiable disease requiring supervised treatment.
dietAdvice: High calorie, high protein diet to combat weight loss. Milk, eggs, meat, legumes, nuts. Vitamin B6 supplement recommended with Isoniazid.

description: A bacterial infection in any part of the urinary system. More common in women. Usually affects the bladder (cystitis) but can spread to kidneys if untreated.
precautions: Wipe front to back after using toilet
Avoid holding urine for long periods

medicines.name: Phenazopyridine (AZO)
medicines.duration: Max 2 days (only for symptom relief, not cure)
medicines.warning: Will turn urine orange/red — this is normal

whenToSeeDoctor: High fever with chills, back/flank pain, nausea/vomiting (signs of kidney involvement), pregnant women with any UTI symptoms, recurrent UTIs (3+ per year).
dietAdvice: Drink plenty of water and unsweetened cranberry juice. Avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods during infection. Probiotics help restore urinary tract flora.

description: Hard deposits of minerals and salts that form inside kidneys. Can cause severe pain as they pass through the urinary tract. Varies from small (pass naturally) to large (requiring intervention).
precautions: Drink at least 3-4 liters of water daily
Reduce salt and animal protein intake
Avoid vitamin C supplements in excess
Follow up with urologist for recurrent stones
whenToSeeDoctor: Fever with chills (infection + obstruction), unable to control pain, stone larger than 6mm (unlikely to pass naturally), only one functioning kidney.
dietAdvice: High water intake is critical. Lemon water helps (citrate prevents calcium stones). Reduce red meat, salt, and oxalate foods.

description: A chronic autoimmune inflammatory disease that primarily affects joints, causing progressive damage if untreated. Can affect multiple organ systems.
precautions: Regular blood tests to monitor disease activity

medicines.name: Methotrexate
medicines.warning: Take folic acid supplement to reduce side effects. Avoid alcohol.

whenToSeeDoctor: Newly swollen or painful joints, inability to perform daily activities, signs of infection while on immunosuppressants, severe medication side effects.
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory diet: fatty fish (omega-3), olive oil, leafy greens, berries, turmeric. Minimize processed foods, red meat, and alcohol.

description: A condition where the thyroid gland produces insufficient thyroid hormone, slowing down metabolism. Very manageable with daily medication.
precautions: Take thyroid medication on empty stomach at same time daily
Regular TSH blood tests every 6-12 months
Avoid taking with calcium or iron supplements (2-4 hour gap)
Inform all doctors about thyroid condition

medicines.name: Levothyroxine (Eltroxin/Thyronorm)
medicines.frequency: Once daily on empty stomach (30-60 min before breakfast)

whenToSeeDoctor: Symptoms not improving on medication, difficulty swallowing, rapid weight changes, palpitations, symptoms of myxedema (severe hypothyroidism).
dietAdvice: Adequate iodine intake (iodized salt, seafood). Limit goitrogens (raw cabbage, broccoli) in large amounts. Selenium-rich foods like Brazil nuts.

description: The most common type of anemia, caused by insufficient iron to produce hemoglobin. Very common in women of reproductive age, pregnant women, and vegetarians.
precautions: Never take iron with tea, coffee, or dairy (reduces absorption)
Take iron with vitamin C to enhance absorption
Check for underlying causes (menstrual blood loss, GI bleeding)

medicines.name: Ferrous Sulfate
medicines.childDose: 3-6 mg/kg/day of elemental iron in divided doses
medicines.frequency: 2-3 times daily between meals or with Vitamin C

whenToSeeDoctor: Hemoglobin below 8 g/dL, symptoms not improving after 4 weeks of treatment, suspected GI bleeding, severe fatigue affecting daily life, during pregnancy.
dietAdvice: Iron-rich foods: red meat, liver, lentils, tofu, spinach, pumpkin seeds. Pair with vitamin C (lemon, orange). Avoid tea/coffee with meals.

description: Inflammation of the sinuses, usually caused by viral infection (most cases) or bacteria. Acute sinusitis lasts less than 4 weeks; chronic lasts over 12 weeks.
precautions: Nasal saline irrigation (neti pot) twice daily

medicines.name: Xylometazoline Nasal Spray (Otrivin)
medicines.warning: Do not use for more than 5 days — causes rebound congestion

medicines.name: Fluticasone Nasal Spray
medicines.childDose: 1 spray per nostril once daily (6+ yrs)

whenToSeeDoctor: High fever, severe headache, swelling around eyes, vision changes, stiff neck, symptoms lasting more than 10 days or worsening after initial improvement.
dietAdvice: Spicy foods and horseradish can help open sinuses. Stay very hydrated. Avoid dairy if mucus is a problem. Warm soups and broths are helpful.

symptoms: redness in the white of the eye
description: Inflammation of the conjunctiva (clear tissue covering the white of the eye). Can be viral, bacterial, or allergic. Infectious types are highly contagious.
precautions: Do not touch or rub eyes
Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup
Discard eye makeup used during infection
Children should stay home from school during infectious period

medicines.name: Chloramphenicol Eye Drops (for bacterial)
medicines.adultDose: 1-2 drops every 2 hours (reduce to 4x/day as improves)

medicines.name: Olopatadine Eye Drops (for allergic)
medicines.duration: As long as allergen exposure continues

whenToSeeDoctor: Severe eye pain, significant change in vision, sensitivity to light, corneal involvement, newborns with eye discharge, symptoms not improving after 5 days.
dietAdvice: Diet doesn't directly affect conjunctivitis recovery. Vitamin A rich foods (carrots, sweet potato) support general eye health.

description: Gastroesophageal reflux disease occurs when stomach acid repeatedly flows back into the food pipe. A very common digestive disorder affecting millions.
precautions: Do not eat 3 hours before bedtime
Elevate head of bed by 6-8 inches
Avoid trigger foods: citrus, tomatoes, chocolate, coffee, alcohol, fatty foods

medicines.name: Antacid (Gelusil/Digene)
medicines.adultDose: 10-20ml or 2 tablets after meals and at bedtime

whenToSeeDoctor: Difficulty swallowing, unexplained weight loss, vomiting blood, black stools, symptoms not responding to medication, symptoms lasting more than 4 weeks.
dietAdvice: Avoid spicy, oily, acidic foods. Eat slowly and chew well. Alkaline foods: bananas, oatmeal, ginger, green vegetables, melons. Avoid late-night eating.

description: A sleep disorder characterized by persistent difficulty with sleep initiation, maintenance, or quality. Can be short-term (acute) or long-term (chronic). Often secondary to anxiety, depression, or lifestyle factors.
precautions: Maintain consistent sleep schedule (same time daily)
Limit screen time 1 hour before bed
Keep bedroom cool, dark, and quiet
Exercise regularly but not near bedtime

medicines.name: Melatonin
medicines.duration: Short-term (2-4 weeks for jet lag or adjustment)

medicines.name: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl/Nytol)
medicines.warning: Only for short-term use. Can cause daytime grogginess.

medicines.name: Zolpidem (Ambien)
medicines.warning: Only take when you have 7-8 hours for sleep. Do not drive after taking.

whenToSeeDoctor: Insomnia lasting more than 3 months, daytime impairment affecting work/safety, associated with depression or anxiety, suspicion of sleep apnea (snoring, gasping).
dietAdvice: Avoid caffeine after 2 PM, limit alcohol, avoid heavy meals near bedtime. Sleep-promoting foods: warm milk, chamomile tea, banana, almonds, oatmeal.

symptoms: aching or stiffness along the spine
worsening pain with prolonged sitting or standing
description: One of the most common medical problems, affecting 8 in 10 people at some point. Most cases are mechanical (muscle strain, poor posture) and resolve within 4-6 weeks.
precautions: Stay active with gentle walking (bed rest worsens outcome)
Apply heat/ice for 15-20 minutes several times daily
Lift objects correctly (bend knees, straight back)
Core strengthening exercises once acute phase subsides

medicines.name: Ibuprofen
medicines.warning: Take with food. Avoid if history of peptic ulcer or kidney disease.

medicines.name: Diclofenac Gel (Topical)
medicines.adultDose: Apply 2-4g to affected area 3-4 times daily

medicines.name: Cyclobenzaprine (Muscle Relaxant)
medicines.warning: Causes drowsiness. Do not drive or operate machinery.

whenToSeeDoctor: Numbness or tingling in legs/feet, bowel or bladder problems, severe pain not improving with medication, pain following an injury, fever with back pain, pain in people over 50.
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory diet: omega-3 fatty acids (fish, flaxseed), turmeric, ginger, cherries. Maintain healthy weight to reduce spinal load. Adequate vitamin D and calcium for bone health.

description: A widespread nutritional deficiency caused by insufficient sun exposure, dietary intake, or absorption. Affects bone health, immune function, and mental health.
precautions: Get 15-30 minutes of midday sun exposure daily (arms and legs exposed)
Take supplements if recommended by doctor
Regular vitamin D level tests (25-OH-D blood test)
Use vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) form for supplementation
Pair with vitamin K2 for bone absorption

medicines.name: Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3)
medicines.adultDose: 60,000 IU weekly for 8-12 weeks (loading), then 2000 IU daily
medicines.frequency: Weekly loading dose, then daily maintenance

whenToSeeDoctor: Level below 20 ng/mL on blood test, bone fractures with minimal trauma, chronic conditions (kidney/liver disease), malabsorption disorders, children with rickets signs.
dietAdvice: Vitamin D-rich foods: fatty fish (salmon, sardines), egg yolks, fortified milk, mushrooms (sun-exposed). Most people need supplements to reach optimal levels.
